text: This is a video where we are going to describe laparoscopic myomectomy techniques for patients having fibroids weighing more than two kgs.
The laparoscopic ports are positioned high up at the level of the xiphoid process to facilitate access for the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon pushes the uterovesical fold down to expose the round ligament, which is visible. Bilateral uterine artery ligation is performed as part of the initial steps in the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
We prefer not to use vasopressin in such situations.
Following bilateral uterine artery ligation, the surgeon makes an incision over the most prominent part of the fibroid using a standard technique.

It doesn't really matter if you have a transverse incision or an oblong incision or an oblique incision or a vertical incision. You need to take the incision which you are most comfortable suturing with.
The surgeon pulls the fibroid out from the underlying capsule formed by the myometrium, demonstrating that the fibroid can be extracted without the use of aqua dissection or vasopressin.
The surgeon dissects a large anterofundal fibroid, approximately eighteen centimeters in size, from the surrounding myometrial tissue.
After removing the large anterofundal fibroid, the surgeon identifies another fibroid located at the posterior cervical region and prepares to dissect it away.
The surgical field shows minimal blood loss in the pouch of Douglas, despite not using vasopressin, as the uterine arteries have been clipped.
The surgeon places two clips over the uterine arteries on both sides, noting that even with large fibroids, the uterine artery size typically does not exceed four to five millimeters.
After the removal of both fibroids, the surgeon performs multi-layered suturing of the myoma bed using quill sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The quill sutures are knotless sutures because of the special barb arrangement along the entire length.
The surgeon performs multi-layer suturing in four layers on the posterior fibroid site, ensuring the myoma bed is closed without hematoma formation.

The surgical field shows minimal blood loss without the use of suction irrigation, as the posterior part of the myoma bed has been sutured. The surgeon proceeds to suture the anterior part of the myoma bed.
After completing multi-layer suturing of both myoma beds, the surgeon uses a morcellator to extract the myoma from the peritoneal cavity.

This is one of the most time consuming and important steps as this approximately in a patient having a fibroid more than two to two point five kgs takes approximately forty five minutes to an hour for the entire thing to perform. Initially, we were using the more safe bag or in bag morcellation. However, of late since the last six to eight months, we've completely abandoned that procedure.
